titleStmt.title: Letter from William Tyrrell Jr. to Elizabeth Tyrrell, 4 November 1917
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: William Tyrrell Jr.

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
The letters of William Upton Tyrrell ('Willie') to his father William J.H. Tyrrell, mother Elizabeth, sister Minnie and brother Robert are a notable example of wartime correspondence. The letters - nearly 200 in total - are mostly written by William to his family while in active service with the Royal Irish Rifles in France. Willie discusses his life and the lives of other soldiers in the trenches, as well as life at Ballindoolin, the family estate.
The family name 'Tyrrell' is sometimes misspelled 'Tyrell' in correspondence to the family.
Summary of the present letter:
The previous day men on both sides of the front line made a great effort to shoot down some geese flying high over head. Not a single bird fell. Parcels have been delayed.

msIdentifier.repository: OPW-Maynooth University Archive & Research Centre at Castletown

correspAction.placeName: Ballindoolin House, Ballindoolin, Edenderry, Kings Co., Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
4/11/17 My dear Mamy. Just a line as usual. This
is Sunday again but of course
here it is just the ame as any
other day. The weather is very
mild and dry but dull &
you would expect all the time
that is was just going to rain
but it never does. There were great flocks of wild
geese came over yesterday very
high up. Every one was having a
pot at them with rifles and I
have nearly heard heaven fire
than was going on. They flew
all along the line but never a one
came down. I believe the boche
fired a machine gun at them. There are great quantities of
partridges all round here to if only
one had a gun.
I was thinking of firing a few bombs
at them to try and get
some. They would be delicious
for dinner. There seems to be a serious
delay in the parcel post. I expect
when the one with the pheasant
comes I will get considerable
notice of it approach. Your letter of the 30th came
yesterday. We get letters
regularly at about 1 for every
day. I am going to send home
my 2 legs as soon as I get
out of the trunches as they are
too small for me for some
unknown reason. I have got
a very good pair of Army
boots in their place. The trenches
are not a bit wet now. Please do not be thinking that
I am needing any of the things
that are in these parcels because
I am not. Extra underwear
& boots I would not wear here
even if I had them. The eatables
are a luxury and not a
necessity - Do not be thinking
I am not getting enough to
eat. It is rather the reverse
I have nothing to do but eat
all day long - It will be very nice if you
can have Bobby out for his birthday.
No news. Every best love to
all from Your most affec Son
Willie _________________
